_preview_note: PREVIEW of the compiled story file. The cascade builds this up one variant per tick from the 14 pending queue entries. Do NOT load this directly — it lives outside the mod data dir on purpose. Final target: src/main/resources/data/dungeontrain/narratives/stories/della_aaro_the_searching_mother.json

letters.label: Letter One
variants:
I fell asleep holding her hand.

I woke up holding nothing.

The seat beside me was still warm.

So I haven't been asleep long. So she can't have gone far.


That's what I keep telling myself.

Della

Philippa.

You fell asleep on my shoulder with the orange book still in your bag. I felt the corner of it against my arm.

Then the train hit a bump. Just one.


And you were gone, and the book was gone, and the warmth stayed.

I'm going to walk every carriage until it gives you back.

You can read this. I taught you that.

Della

letters.label: Letter Two
variants:
I've stopped crying. Crying is for when nothing can be done. Plenty can be done.

I have a notebook. I number the carriages and mark the ones I've searched.


I'll find my girl the same way.

And her father, if he made it aboard.

Della

Philippa,

Your father went ahead to the train weeks ago, to find us a way through. Then the raiders came, and it was only us.

We ran. We came here to find him.


So I'm looking for two faces now. Yours, and his.

Two is not harder than one. It is twice the reason to keep my eyes open.

I cross out the carriages. I don't cross out the days.

Della

letters.label: Letter Three
variants:
I found a paper today.

Three words in a child's hand, and I knew them before I read them. I taught that hand its letters.


It was hers. Pip's. It said she is looking for me.

So she is alive. She is awake. She is moving, and she is looking, same as I am.


The paper was not fresh. She passed through before me. I am behind her.

But behind is not lost. Behind, I can follow.

Della

Philippa. I found your note.

You wrote that you are looking for me. I have read it forty times. I have it in my coat.


You are out there, and you are still you — still looking, still walking, not sitting down to wait. That is my girl.


So here is what we do now. You leave your papers. I follow them. I am closer than I was yesterday, even if the train says otherwise.

Keep writing, Pip. I am reading every word.

Della

letters.label: Letter Four
variants:
There is a man on this train who wears a yellow coat.

I have seen men like him before — after long marches, after sieges. The train is only a longer siege.


He told me the train does not stop. He has stopped, though, in every way that counts. He mostly sleeps now.

But he looked at me when I described her. And he nodded.


He saw her. A small girl, he said, asking everyone for her mother. Would not sit, would not stay.

That is twice now the train has told me she is alive. I will not get tired of hearing it.

Della

Philippa, today I met a man in a yellow coat.

He has ridden this train longer than a person should. The train has worn him thin. But there is still kindness in him.


He remembered you. A girl asking after her mother, he said, who would not sit down. He smiled when he said it. The first time I have seen this train make anyone smile.


I asked him about your father too. About Tomas. He shook his head. Nothing.

The train will give me back one of you. I refuse to believe it cannot be both.

Della

letters.label: Letter Five
variants:
I have a friend now. Her name is Soren.

I had forgotten what it costs to do this alone, until I stopped having to.


She has been on the train longer than I have years. She knows its moods. She gives away food she cannot spare and calls it nothing.

We search together now. Two sets of eyes.


She thinks I push too hard. I think she has stopped pushing at all.

For now that is a thing we can laugh about. For now we walk the same direction.

Della

Philippa, I want you to know about Soren.

She found me at a low hour and did not leave. She is the first good thing this train has handed me.


She has been aboard longer than anyone I have met. She talks about a door that leads off the train. She wants me to want it.

I tell her I want only one thing, and it has your name.


When I find you, you will meet her. You will like her, I think. She knows where to find the most delicious apples.

Hold on to that, Pip. There are good people even here.

Della

letters.label: Letter Six
variants:
Pip's papers led into a dark stretch. The kind even Soren would not enter.

She told me to turn back. I told her the trail goes forward, so I go forward.


We said hard things. Then the floor gave way beneath me, and I went down into the dark between the carriages. The last thing I heard was Soren screaming my name.


But I survived. I caught a rail and climbed back up, bruised and breathing.

Not three carriages back, where I fell. With the train moving, surely not ahead.

Somewhere I had yet to travel.


I can't get back to her. The dark does not give second chances.

Whatever she believes became of me down there, I'll never get the chance to tell her otherwise.

The trail goes forward. So do I.

Della

Philippa,

Your trail led into a dark stretch. She begged me not to follow it. I followed it. You were at the end of it, or might be.


We argued. The last word I gave her was a sharp one. Then the floor opened under me and I fell into the dark between the carriages, with her screaming above me.


I lived, Pip. I caught hold and climbed back, far down the train. Soren saw none of it — only me dropping into the black and not coming back.

She likely thinks I'm dead, and I can't tell her she's wrong.


If I do not meet her again, at least there is another set of eyes looking for you now.

Della

letters.label: Letter Seven
variants:
I've stopped counting days. Not because I gave up — because the number stopped mattering and the looking did not.


Tomas's trail went cold a long way back. No papers. No word. I keep his name in my mouth so it doesn't get lost too.

Pip's trail I still have. Faint, but mine to follow.


I know now there may be no end to this train, and no end to the walking. I've made my peace with that. I have not made peace with stopping.

So I won't stop.


I leave my own papers now, in the carriages everyone passes. If I can't catch her, she can catch me.


A mother and a daughter, walking the same endless train, leaving each other words.

We will cross. I have decided it.

Della

Philippa, I'm leaving this where everyone passes — the way you taught me, without knowing you taught me.


I'm still behind you. I've read your papers. You're still walking, still you, and that's all I need to keep walking too.

Your father I have not found. Hold his name with me. Tomas.


I don't know how long this train is. I've decided it doesn't matter. I'll walk it as long as it takes, and longer. I am closer than it wants me to be.


You wrote once that mums find things.

You were right. Keep writing, Pip — I am reading every word.

I will find you.

Della
